title: Know When to Look: Parametric-Retrieval Agreement as a Calibration Signal for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
description: We propose PRA-Score, a confidence metric for RAG systems that measures the agreement between an LLM's parametric answer (without retrieval) and its retrieval-augmented answer. The individual PRA signals (exact match, token F1, NLI entailment, token probability delta) are fully training-free; a lightweight logistic regression step combines them with existing confidence signals for state-of-the-art selective prediction. Unlike prior work (PAIRS, SUGAR, Self-Routing RAG) that uses parametric-retrieval comparison for retrieval triggering/routing, PRA-Score targets post-generation calibration -- assessing how reliable the RAG answer is after generation.
motivation: RAG systems improve factual accuracy but their effect on confidence calibration is poorly understood. Retrieved context can make LLMs overconfident, especially when retrieval introduces noise or contradictions (Ozaki et al., 2025; URAG benchmark, Nguyen et al., 2026). Current uncertainty methods (token probability, self-consistency, semantic entropy) were designed for parametric-only LLMs and do not account for RAG-specific failure modes. While PAIRS (Li et al., 2025), SUGAR (Zubkova et al., 2025), and Self-Routing RAG (Zhao et al., 2025) use parametric signals to decide *whether* to retrieve, and NAACL (Liu et al., 2026) requires fine-tuning for noise-aware calibration, no method uses parametric-retrieval agreement for *training-free, post-generation confidence estimation and selective prediction* in a black-box compatible manner.
proposed_approach: PRA-Score computes the semantic agreement between two answers: one generated without retrieval (parametric) and one generated with retrieved context (RAG). We investigate four agreement measures: exact match, token F1, NLI-based entailment probability, and token probability delta (difference in mean log-probs between parametric and RAG passes). The individual signals are fully training-free. For optimal combination with existing confidence signals (token probability, self-consistency, etc.), we use a lightweight logistic regression meta-predictor fitted on ~500 labeled examples -- analogous to standard Platt scaling. We test across model scales from 7B to 70B (4-bit quantized) to assess whether PRA-Score's discriminative power degrades as parametric knowledge improves. We also include an honest cost-benefit analysis, reporting AUROC improvement per additional inference pass.

related_work.title: PAIRS: Parametric-Verified Adaptive Information Retrieval and Selection for Efficient RAG
related_work.difference: PAIRS uses parametric-retrieval agreement to decide whether to *bypass retrieval* (efficiency); PRA-Score uses it for *post-generation confidence estimation* (reliability). PAIRS compares two parametric outputs (direct vs. pseudo-context); PRA compares parametric vs. real retrieval-augmented output.

related_work.title: SUGAR: Leveraging Contextual Confidence for Smarter Retrieval
related_work.difference: SUGAR uses semantic entropy from parametric generation as a retrieval trigger. PRA-Score measures agreement between parametric and retrieval-augmented answers for post-generation calibration -- a different pipeline stage and optimization target.

related_work.title: Self-Routing RAG: Binding Selective Retrieval with Knowledge Verbalization
related_work.difference: Self-Routing RAG casts retrieval as knowledge source selection within a single forward pass, requiring fine-tuning. PRA-Score is training-free at the signal level and targets post-generation calibration rather than routing.

related_work.title: Rethinking LLM Parametric Knowledge as Post-retrieval Confidence for Dynamic Retrieval and Reranking (CBDR)
related_work.difference: CBDR uses LLM hidden states for confidence measurement (requires model internals). PRA-Score works at the output level, making it applicable to black-box models. Our TPD variant bridges toward CBDR while remaining output-accessible.

related_work.title: NAACL: Noise-AwAre Verbal Confidence Calibration for LLMs in RAG Systems
related_work.difference: NAACL requires supervised fine-tuning on ~2K examples and model weight access. PRA-Score is training-free (individual signals) and black-box compatible.

related_work.title: URAG: A Benchmark for Uncertainty Quantification in Retrieval-Augmented Large Language Models
related_work.difference: URAG benchmarks uncertainty across RAG methods but does not propose new confidence signals. Their finding that retrieval amplifies overconfident errors directly motivates PRA-Score as a detection mechanism.

related_work.title: Understanding the Impact of Confidence in Retrieval Augmented Generation: A Case Study in the Medical Domain
related_work.difference: Ozaki et al. confirm RAG increases overconfidence in the medical domain. PRA-Score formalizes this observation into a general-purpose calibration signal.

related_work.title: Confidence Improves Self-Consistency in LLMs (CISC)
related_work.difference: CISC weights reasoning chains by confidence for improved majority voting in parametric-only settings. PRA-Score compares across two knowledge sources (parametric vs. retrieval) rather than weighting chains from one source.

related_work.title: Self-RAG: Learning to Retrieve, Generate, and Critique through Self-Reflection
related_work.difference: Self-RAG requires fine-tuning with special reflection tokens; PRA-Score is training-free (individual signals) and model-agnostic.

related_work.title: Semantic Uncertainty: Linguistic Invariances for Uncertainty Estimation in Natural Language Generation
related_work.difference: Semantic entropy clusters sampled answers from one generation mode; PRA-Score compares answers across two knowledge sources (parametric vs. retrieval).

related_work.title: SelfCheckGPT: Zero-Resource Black-Box Hallucination Detection for Generative Large Language Models
related_work.difference: SelfCheckGPT checks consistency across multiple samples of the same generation mode; PRA-Score specifically contrasts parametric vs. retrieval-augmented generation.

related_work.title: Knowledge Conflicts for LLMs: A Survey
related_work.difference: Surveys knowledge conflicts but does not propose using parametric-retrieval disagreement as a confidence/calibration signal.

hypothesis: The agreement between an LLM's parametric answer and its retrieval-augmented answer (PRA-Score) is a strong predictor of answer correctness that captures complementary information to existing uncertainty methods. Specifically: (1) PRA-Score achieves competitive AUROC for selective prediction compared to existing methods, (2) combining PRA-Score with existing signals improves selective prediction performance by at least 2 AUROC points over the best individual signal, (3) PRA-Score's signal remains useful across model scales (7B-70B), and (4) PRA-Score provides better AUROC-per-inference-pass than self-consistency methods requiring multiple samples.

success_criteria.strong_confirmation: PRA-Score (NLI variant) achieves top-2 AUROC among individual confidence signals on at least 2/3 datasets, AND combination with existing signals improves AUROC by >= 2 points
success_criteria.moderate_confirmation: PRA-Score captures complementary information (combination improves over best individual signal on majority of settings), even if PRA alone is not the strongest individual signal
success_criteria.refutation: PRA-Score provides no additional information beyond existing methods, or parametric-retrieval agreement is too noisy to be useful as a calibration signal

computational_requirements.note: Inference-based, no LLM training required. Two forward passes per question plus baseline methods. 70B model via 4-bit quantization (~35GB VRAM) on NQ subset only.
